7.Let $F_{n}=x^{2}.\mathrm{~I f~}n\equiv1$ $n\equiv1\pmod{4}$ but $n\;\neq\;1$ ,write $n\:=\:1+2\cdot3^{l}\cdot k$ ,with $k\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that $2\mid k,3\nmid k$ . 8.Apply Gauss’lemma, as was done in Example 12.27, or the quadratic reciprocity law.

core_content.markdown:
3. Start by multiplying equality $a x^{2}+b x y+c y^{2}=n$ by 4a and completing squares.



4.Firstshowthatifsuchxanddidexis,thentheywouldberelatively prime with122. Then,use the resultof the previous problem nto conclude that 17 should be a quadratic residue modulo 61. Finally, apply the quadratic a 

5. For item (a), if every prime divisor of $2b^{2}+3$ was congruent $\upiota\uprho\pm1$ modulo 8,we would have $2b^{2}+3\equiv\pm1$ (mod 8); show that this is impossible. For (b, if $(2b^{2}+3)\mid(a^{2}-2)$ and p is as in (a), then $a^{2}\equiv2\left({\bmod{p}}\right)$ 1, so that 2 would be a quadratic residue modulo p; use the result of Example 12.277to reach a contradiction.



6.Let $L_{n}=2x^{2}$ . If n is odd, use item (b) of Problem 11, page 252, together with the fact that $L_{n}$ is even, to show that $n\equiv3\mathrm{o r}9(\bmod12)$ . Then, apply several times item (e) of this same problem to get $L_{n}\equiv L_{3}\;\mathrm{o r}\;L_{9}\equiv4(\mathrm{m o d}8)$ and show that this gives $x^{2}\equiv2\left({\stackrel{\rightharpoonup}{\bmod}}4\right)$ , which is impossible. Now, assuming that n is even, consider the following three separate cases:$n\equiv0\pmod{4}$ $n\equiv6\left(\bmod8\right)\mathrm{o r}n\equiv2\left(\bmod8\right).\mathrm{I f}n\equiv0\left(\bmod4\right)$ ,write $n=2{\cdot}3^{l}{\cdot}k$ ,with $k\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that $2\mid k,3\nmid k$ . Argue as in the proof of Example 12.25 to obtain $L_{n}\equiv$ $-L_{0}=-2(\bmod L_{k})$ and, hence,$x^{2}\equiv-1\left(\bmod L_{k}\right)$ . Show that this furnishes a contradiction, exactly astheoneobtained inthe proofof Example12.2.If $n\equiv6({\bmod{8}})$ but $n=6$ (note that $L_{6}=18=2\cdot3^{2})$ J,write $n=\widetilde{6}+2\cdot3^{l}\cdot k$ with $k\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that 4 | k and $3\nmid k$ . Use item (d) of Problem 13, page 253, to obtain $L_{n}\equiv-L_{6}=-18(\bmod L_{k})$ ', and hence $x^{2}\equiv-9\left(\bmod L_{k}\right)$ . Also as in the proof of Example 12.25, conclude that one can take a prime divisor  of $L_{k}$ such that $p\equiv3({\bmod{4}})$ ,so that $x^{2}\equiv-9\left({\bmod{p}}\right)$ . Then, show that $3\nmid L_{k}$ ，so that gcd $(p,9)=1$ and, thus,$\textstyle{\big(}{\frac{-9}{p}}{\big)}={\big(}{\frac{9}{p}}{\big)}{\overbrace{{\big(}{\frac{-1}{p}}{\big)}}}{\overbrace{=}}{\big(}{\frac{-1}{p}}{\big)}=-1$ . Finally, in the case $n\equiv2\left({\bmod{8}}\right)$ , just note that $L_{-n}\stackrel{\iota}{=}L_{n}\mathrm{a n d}-n\stackrel{\iota}{\equiv}6\pmod{8}$ 「

7.Let $F_{n}=x^{2}.\mathrm{~I f~}n\equiv1$ $n\equiv1\pmod{4}$ but $n\;\neq\;1$ ,write $n\:=\:1+2\cdot3^{l}\cdot k$ ,with $k\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that $2\mid k,3\nmid k$ . Use item (c) of Problem 13, page 253, to get $F_{n}\equiv-F_{1}=-1(\bmod L_{k})$ ; then, arrive at a contradiction, exactly as in the proof of Example 12.25. If $n\equiv3\pmod{4}$ ,use the fact that $F_{-n}\:=\:F_{n}$ and $-n\equiv1({\bmod{4}})$ 1. If n is even, write $n~=~2m$ and apply item (a) of Problem 13, page 253 to get $F_{n}~=~F_{m}L_{m}$ . Then, distinguish two distinct subcases: if gcd $(n,3)=1$ ,use Problem 12, page 252 to conclude that $L_{m}$ is also a perfect square; then, apply the result of Example 12.25. If $\operatorname*{g c d}(n,3)=3$ ,use Problem 12, page 252, again to conclude that $L_{m}$ is twice a perfect square;then, apply the result of the previous problem.



8.Apply Gauss’lemma, as was done in Example 12.27, or the quadratic reciprocity law.



9.公司添, if every prime divisor of $2^{n}-1$ was congruent $\upiota\uprho\pm1$ modulo 12,we would have $2^{n}-1\equiv\pm1\:(\bmod12)$ ; show that this is impossible. For (b), if $(2^{n}-1)\nmid(3^{m}-1)$ and p is as in (a), then $3^{m}\equiv1\pmod{p}$ or, which is the same,$3^{m\dot{+}1}\equiv3\left(\bmod p\right)$ ; since $m+1$ is even, it would follow that 3 would be
